ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tilapia food product class; no specific entry for deep-fried red tilapia with vermicelli exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked tilapia as the primary protein component. No composite dish code for fried tilapia with vermicelli exists in FoodEx2; component-level anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for fried tilapia with vermicelli dish found; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: gluten (possible — fish sauce may contain wheat)

serving.description: 1 whole fried fish with a side of vermicelli and accompanying sauce/vegetables
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 650 g, comprising a whole deep-fried red tilapia (roughly 350–400 g edible portion), a side of boiled rice vermicelli (approximately 150 g), fresh herbs, and a fish-sauce-based dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tilapia, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15262) and Rice noodles, cooked (NDB 20113); composite dish estimate weighted by ingredient proportion
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for the composite dish 'Fried Red Tilapia with Vermicelli'. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries: deep-fried tilapia (~55% by weight of edible dish), cooked rice vermicelli (~25%), fresh herbs (~10%), and fish-sauce dipping sauce (~10%). The input record's per-100g values (energy 146 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 8.46 g, carbohydrate 7.69 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium of 276.9 mg/100g reflects fish sauce contribution and is plausible. All values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tilapia (cooked, dry heat) and rice noodles (cooked).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli (GI ~53, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: The GI of the composite dish is dominated by the rice vermicelli component. Rice vermicelli has a published GI of approximately 53 (low–medium boundary) per the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The deep-fried tilapia and fresh herbs contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect the dish GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (49.9 g from 650 g × 7.69 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 26.4 g. This places the dish in the moderate-to-high GL range per serving, driven by the large portion size rather than a high GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 276.9 mg/100g; estimated 1,800 mg per full 650 g serving from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request the dipping sauce on the side and limit fish sauce quantity, as a full serving may contribute approximately 75–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; increasing the proportion of fresh herbs and vegetables can help offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a moderate-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~53); glycemic load per full 650 g serving is approximately 26 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should practise portion control on the vermicelli component, as the glycemic load per full serving is moderate-to-high; the high protein content from tilapia (10 g/100g) helps slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose rise, but the refined carbohydrate load should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat (8.46 g/100g) and saturated fat (1.54 g/100g); high sodium from fish sauce; trans fat present at 0.08 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, as deep-frying increases caloric density and fat content; the sodium from fish sauce is a secondary concern for blood pressure management, and the small trans fat content from frying oil should be noted; tilapia itself provides lean protein and some omega-3 fatty acids, which are beneficial.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may worsen joint inflammation; tilapia has a less favourable omega-6 to omega-3 ratio compared to fatty fish
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider requesting the tilapia steamed or grilled rather than deep-fried to reduce AGE formation; substituting tilapia with salmon or mackerel would improve the omega-3 to omega-6 ratio and provide greater anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Red Tilapia with Vermicelli is a Vietnamese-inspired dish popular in Singapore, featuring a whole red tilapia that is deep-fried until the skin is golden and crispy while the flesh remains moist and flaky. The fish is served alongside boiled rice vermicelli noodles (bún), a generous arrangement of fresh herbs such as mint, Vietnamese coriander, and Thai basil, sliced cucumber, bean sprouts, and a tangy fish-sauce-based dipping sauce (nước chấm) made with lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The dish delivers a contrast of textures — crispy fried fish skin against soft noodles and crunchy fresh vegetables — and a balance of savoury, sour, sweet, and spicy flavours characteristic of Vietnamese cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 146 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 7.7 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 277 mg primarily from fish sauce. The glycemic index is approximately 53 (low–medium), driven by the rice vermicelli component. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to fish sauce sodium, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the refined carbohydrate load from vermicelli, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to deep-frying increasing fat content and the presence of small amounts of trans fat from frying oil. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried tilapia and cooked rice vermicelli, cross-checked against the input record values which are broadly consistent. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate as the dish composition (herb quantity, sauce volume, oil absorption) introduces variability.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese fried tilapia with vermicelli dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not provide a matching composite dish entry with full micronutrient breakdown. Trans fat of 0.08 g/100g is retained from the input record as a plausible estimate for deep-fried preparation using standard cooking oil; this is consistent with USDA data for deep-fried fish products. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 8.46, carbohydrate_g 7.69) are non-zero and reflect the composite dish composition. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (53) × carbohydrate per serving (650 g × 7.69 g/100g = 49.99 g) ÷ 100 = 26.4 g. The dish is classified as Vietnamese-inspired but is commonly served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for both Vietnamese (mandatory per cuisine rule) and Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Halal status is assumed based on standard preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
